name: Cyprus Haircut
description: The 'Cyprus Haircut' theory, primarily circulating within alternative history circles, posits that ancient Cypriot hairstyles, specifically short, geometric cuts depicted in archaeological finds (statues, pottery), are evidence of advanced, lost knowledge. Proponents claim these hairstyles were not merely fashion statements, but represent encoded maps, astronomical charts, or even sophisticated medical techniques related to cranial alignment and energy flow. They draw parallels to similar geometric patterns found in other ancient cultures, suggesting a shared, now-forgotten global civilization. The theory often links the haircuts to the island's historical connections to Atlantis and other lost civilizations. Some interpretations suggest these styles were not literal haircuts but symbolic representations worn by priests or initiates. Mainstream archaeology and art history reject these interpretations. They argue that the hairstyles are simply reflections of contemporary fashion and artistic conventions. The geometric patterns are attributed to the limitations of the materials used (clay, stone) and the artistic skills of the time. Furthermore, the lack of supporting textual evidence or scientific validation for the claimed encoded information renders the theory speculative. Critics point out the tendency to cherry-pick evidence and misinterpret artistic representations within a modern framework. The theory often appears in online forums and alternative history documentaries, contributing to its continued circulation despite the absence of scholarly support.
subtitle: Encoded knowledge in ancient hairstyles?

claims.claim: Ancient Cypriot haircuts encode astronomical charts.
claims.evidence: Geometric patterns resembling constellations on statues.
claims.criticism: Patterns are artistic conventions, not precise astronomical representations.

claims.claim: The 'Cyprus Haircut' represents lost medical knowledge related to cranial alignment.
claims.evidence: Speculative interpretations linking head shape to energy flow.
claims.criticism: No scientific basis for claims about cranial alignment and 'energy flow'.

claims.claim: The hairstyles are evidence of a shared, advanced ancient civilization.
claims.evidence: Similar geometric patterns in other ancient cultures.
claims.criticism: Superficial similarities are insufficient evidence; convergent development is more likely.

keyFigures.name: Graham Hancock
keyFigures.role: Author and proponent of alternative historical theories.

keyFigures.name: Colin Renfrew
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist and critic of diffusionist theories.

timeline.event: Depiction of geometric hairstyles in art.
Continued circulation of the theory online.

sources.author: Feder, Kenneth L.
sources.work: Frauds, Myths, and Mysteries: Science and Pseudoscience in Archaeology
sources.title: Feder, Kenneth L.
